point_001: The novelty of the proposed SPE method is limited, as it essentially averages embeddings from multiple pre-trained classifiers without introducing a fundamentally new mechanism or insight beyond ensemble averaging.
point_002: Key design choices lack justification: why exactly 7 datasets were selected, why averaging (rather than other aggregation strategies) is used, and whether some datasets contribute more than others to the final representation.
point_003: The threshold of 2.5 used for the rASR metric is not adequately justified; a score of 3 corresponds to only 'slight similarity,' raising questions about whether this cutoff is appropriate for measuring semantic preservation.
point_004: The paper's motivation is undermined by a gap between the identified problem (antonym substitutions confusing existing encoders) and the proposed solution, which does not explicitly address antonym disambiguation.
point_005: The evaluation may be unfair because SPE is trained on classification datasets (NLI, SST-2, Yelp, etc.), meaning its similarity scores could reflect classification boundaries rather than genuine semantic similarity, conflating the two concepts.
point_006: SPE is not evaluated on standard sentence similarity benchmarks (e.g., SemEval STS tasks), so its validity as a general semantic similarity metric versus a task-specific classifier proxy is never established.
point_007: The experimental coverage of adversarial attack methods is narrow; the paper does not test whether SPE improves quality for other prominent attacks such as BERT-Attack, CLARE, or SememePSO.
point_008: Several relevant prior word-level adversarial attack works are not discussed or compared against (e.g., Alzantot et al. 2018, Ren et al. 2019, Dong et al. 2021).
point_009: The paper conflates word-level and sentence-level semantic preservation without clearly distinguishing them, even though these can diverge significantly in practice.
point_010: The complexity formula in Equation (1) is questionable: it is unclear why complexity depends on vocabulary size rather than sentence length.
point_011: Inconsistent experimental setup is unexplained: distilBERT is used as the victim model on the Rotten Tomatoes dataset while RoBERTa is used on all other datasets, with no rationale provided.
point_012: The paper lacks deeper qualitative analysis showing which specific types of substitutions are filtered out by SPE, making it difficult to understand the mechanism behind any observed improvements.
point_013: The generalizability of SPE to domains or tasks beyond those represented in its training datasets is not discussed, limiting confidence in its broader applicability as a semantic checker.
